learned: Every design decision is a behavioral claim. Where the button sits claims where people look; the empty state claims what a lost person needs; the progress bar claims how effort feels. Most teams make these claims on gut feeling and a deadline. The whole discipline of this book is making them with an actual model of how people think. Design without that model is decoration that works until someone uses it.
apply: Before any review, restate each major design choice as the behavioral claim it makes: 'we believe users will…'
Keep a running list of which claims are verified by observation vs. assumed
Hire for and reward curiosity about users' minds, not just craft and polish
flags: Decisions justified purely by aesthetics or 'feels right'
Nobody in the room can say which user behavior a choice depends on
question: What claim about human behavior does this design make, and how do we know it's true?

learned: The false consensus effect means my own reaction is always over-weighted: I unconsciously treat 'what I would do' as 'what most users would do', and explain away people who differ as edge cases. Color burned $41M learning this: a team that understood its product perfectly shipped it to people who couldn't even tell what it was for. The dangerous part is that this bias feels like experience, not bias.
apply: Name the source of every choice out loud: observed user behavior, or my own preference?
Recruit test participants who are demonstrably unlike the team
Ban 'not our target user' as an explanation for a failed test until someone proves it with data
when: Cutting a feature you personally wouldn't use
Writing labels and copy that feel 'obvious'
Any 'users will get it' moment in a review
flags: The room explains the participant instead of the screen
Confidence rising with zero new user evidence
question: Would someone who has never seen this product land here and see what I see, right now, without a tour?

learned: Impostor syndrome isn't a private feeling, it's a product defect: it edits the work before anyone sees it. I shrink the ambitious option, pad the deck with safe choices, and the user receives the timid version. The fix isn't confidence, it's process: make the bold option's existence non-negotiable so my mood can't delete it.
apply: Always bring the ambitious option to review alongside the safe one, clearly framed
Track ideas killed before review; if that number is high, the filter is fear not judgment
Pair designers on early concepts so shrinkage is visible to someone
when: Preparing any presentation of your own work
flags: The 'crazy version' exists only in a private file
Scope quietly shrinking without a decision meeting
question: What did I already remove from this work before anyone else got to judge it?

learned: Once identity fuses with the artifact, feedback becomes threat and cognitive dissonance handles the rest: I defend, rationalize, and stop learning. Microsoft's Kin shipped partly because too many people needed it to be good. The tell is the reflex to explain rather than to ask. Great practitioners hold their work like a hypothesis, not a self-portrait.
apply: Present work as an experiment: 'we tried X to get Y, here's what we're unsure about'
When critiqued, ask one clarifying question before any defense
Rotate who presents the work so ownership stays with the team, not one ego
when: Post-launch retros on your own feature
Deciding whether to kill your own idea
flags: Answering critique with the design's backstory
Feeling relieved when a test gets cancelled
question: If someone else had made this exact thing, what would I say about it?

learned: Fluency masquerades as quality: the longer I look at my own design, the clearer and better it feels, independent of whether it is. Google Wave felt world-changing from inside the building. My own sense of 'this is obviously good' is the least trustworthy signal I own, because it strengthens with exposure rather than with evidence.
apply: Fix a cadence of outside eyes: every two weeks, someone new sees the work cold
Ask newcomers to narrate what they see before you explain a single thing
Write predictions before tests: 'users will do X here' — then check your calibration
when: Weeks-long projects with a stable team
Evaluating your own redesign against the old version
flags: 'It's intuitive' said by the person who built it
No one outside the team has seen it in a month
question: When did someone outside this team last watch this cold, and what surprised us?

learned: Mere exposure inflates my taste for whatever I've stared at: the logo 'grows on' the team because repetition feels like liking. Real taste is trained by volume and variety of great work; fake taste is trained by looking at my own file for a month. MySpace made clutter feel normal to millions the same way. First impressions are a non-renewable resource: the team spent theirs weeks ago.
apply: Never let the team's Nth impression outvote a user's first impression
Collect fresh-eyes reactions in the first 30 seconds, separately from team opinions
Keep a reference library of excellent work and compare against it, not against yesterday's iteration
when: Anything the team calls 'grown on us'
flags: 'It grew on me' as an argument
Judging quality without any external benchmark
question: Are we judging this design, or our familiarity with it?

learned: Creativity is throughput, not lightning. Divergent thinking happens in the making: rough thing → reaction → better thing. IDEO's shopping cart took five days of building, not five days of waiting for the muse. Input volume also caps output: you can only connect what you've collected. Bad ideas aren't waste, they're fertilizer — the cost of a good idea is a pile of bad ones.
apply: Timebox concepting to making: N rough artifacts by Friday, not 'explore the space'
Feed the input side deliberately: study products, papers, patterns outside your niche
Quota bad ideas on purpose: 10 sketches where 8 are allowed to be terrible
when: Brainstorms that are all talk, no artifacts
flags: Empty canvas plus a full calendar of ideation meetings
First idea being polished instead of second idea being sought
question: What did we make this week that we could react to?

learned: Yerkes-Dodson: pressure sharpens until it doesn't, then it narrows. Under deadline stress, teams stop questioning, treat risks as inconveniences, and grab the fastest answer. NASA knew Challenger wasn't ready and the schedule won anyway. Deadline decisions need MORE structure, not less, because that's exactly when judgment is worst.
apply: Near deadlines, write down what you're explicitly choosing not to question
Assign one person per crunch to ask the slow questions; protect them from the schedule
Pre-commit go/no-go criteria before the pressure arrives, then honor them
when: Any 'we'll fix it after ship' conversation
flags: Risk items relabeled as polish items
Dissent drying up as the date approaches
question: Which warning are we currently reframing as an inconvenience?

learned: Design fixation: the first sketch silently becomes the template, and 'exploration' degrades into variations of it. Zune iterated hard inside the iPod's frame and lost inside it too. The first idea also smuggles in decisions nobody made consciously — layout logic, entry point, metaphor. Alternatives only count if they break the skeleton, not the skin.
apply: Force three structurally different concepts before iterating on any (different layout logic, entry point, metaphor)
Start one exploration from a different user moment than the 'obvious' one
In reviews, ask which decisions were inherited from sketch #1 rather than made
when: Right after the first promising sketch
Concept phases that converged suspiciously fast
flags: All explorations share one skeleton
Loving V1 too much to ask what V0 assumed
question: Which decisions in this design were actually made, and which were just… kept?

learned: The curse of knowledge is one-way glass: once I know how it works, I literally cannot experience my product as a newcomer, because my brain auto-fills every gap. Photoshop was built by experts for experts and spent decades bolting on on-ramps. My intuition about learnability is worthless; only a genuine beginner's behavior counts.
apply: Put a true first-timer in front of every release; watch, don't guide
Write onboarding copy with a rule: zero terms that require existing product knowledge
Keep a 'beginner questions' log and treat repeats as bugs, not user error
flags: 'Everyone knows what that icon means'
Team members can't remember not understanding the product
question: Was a person who's never seen this in the room before we called it easy?

learned: Confirmation bias runs my research unless I rig the process against it: I notice the quotes that agree, trust the sessions that flatter, and explain away the rest. Segway heard warnings and heard them as marketing problems. The only defense is deciding in advance what would prove me wrong — research that cannot fail is a permission slip, not research.
apply: Write falsification criteria before fieldwork: 'we're wrong if users do/say X'
Have someone uninvested moderate the sessions
Present disconfirming evidence first in every readout
when: Validating a direction leadership already loves
Reading survey results after you have a favorite
Any research done after the decision
flags: Every study conveniently supports the roadmap
Negative signals reframed as messaging issues
question: What result did we agree beforehand would kill this idea, and did we look for it?

learned: Users verdict the product in ~50ms, before reading a word, and that thin-slice judgment colors everything after: trust, quality, even perceived usability. Airbnb's turnaround was partly about fixing what people saw first. The visible layer does the first trust work; treating visual polish as 'just aesthetics' misunderstands what it buys.
apply: Run five-second tests on key screens: flash it, ask what it is and if it looks trustworthy
Prioritize the first screen's craft budget above interior screens
Audit what the first glance communicates: category, quality, credibility
flags: 'Users will look past the rough edges once they try it'
First impression tested only on people who already know the product
question: What does this screen say in its first fifty milliseconds?

learned: There is no neutral screen: the default effect and framing mean whatever is pre-selected, first, or highlighted is a recommendation most users will follow without noticing they were steered. That's power with a direction — it either serves the user or exploits them. The starting state is the product's strongest opinion.
apply: Audit every default quarterly: whose interest does this serve?
Frame options by user outcome, not by what maximizes our metric
Make the recommended path genuinely recommendable — best for most users, defensible out loud
flags: Defaults chosen by revenue impact alone
'They can always change it' as the justification
question: If users saw exactly why this default was chosen, would they thank us?

learned: Affordances make actions possible; signifiers make them findable. Flat design stripped the signifiers and usability quietly fell — beautiful screens where nobody knows what's tappable. When users miss the button, designers blame attention; the real culprit is a missing cue. Discoverability is the design's job, never the user's.
apply: Run silent click tests: static screen, 'point at everything you think is clickable'
Give every interactive element at least one honest cue: border, shadow, color, underline
Re-add signifiers wherever analytics show features nobody finds
when: Features with great function but poor adoption
Card-based layouts where tap targets are ambiguous
flags: Interactive and static elements styled identically
'Users will discover it eventually'
question: Could a stranger circle everything clickable on this screen and be right?

learned: 'Intuitive' is a lie of vocabulary: nothing is intuitive, things are familiar. A design feels obvious when it matches schemas the user already carries, and baffling when it asks them to learn a new one on the spot. Experts and novices carry different schemas, so 'intuitive for whom' is the only honest question. Novelty is a tax; spend it where it pays.
apply: Inventory the products your users live in daily; borrow those conventions shamelessly
Spend novelty on your one differentiating interaction, convention everywhere else
When someone says 'intuitive', make them name the pattern it borrows from
when: Any novel gesture or interaction proposal
flags: 'Intuitive' in a sentence with no referenced convention
Demo-day novelty confusing every first-timer
question: Familiar to which user, from which product they already know?

learned: Cognitive load is a budget the user brings, partly pre-spent by their day, and every field, label, and decision draws it down. When the balance hits zero they don't complain, they leave. TurboTax turned dreaded complexity into one question at a time. Simplicity isn't minimal pixels, it's minimal simultaneous demands.
apply: Count decisions per screen; set a budget and enforce it in review
Ask one thing at a time in flows; defer everything deferrable
Default aggressively: users should succeed by only touching what they care about
flags: Screens that need instructions to survive
Optional fields nobody remembers the reason for
question: What is the maximum this screen asks a tired person to hold in their head?

learned: Predictive processing: users are always guessing what comes next, and every kept promise builds speed while every broken pattern forces conscious checking. Vista showed the bill for internal inconsistency. Consistency isn't tidiness, it's the user's clock speed — and designers can't see their own inconsistencies because they know the intent behind each screen.
apply: Same action, same place, same words — audit across every surface quarterly
Keep one source of truth for patterns and make deviation require sign-off
Test cross-screen journeys, not single screens, to expose broken predictions
flags: Primary buttons wandering between screens
Same concept named differently in two places
question: Where does this product break a promise its own patterns made?

learned: The forgetting curve guarantees users lose most of the interface within days — then blame themselves, which is worse than blaming us because shame quietly ends usage. Memory is a design dependency I don't control. The product must carry the memory: recognition over recall, context at the point of action, state made visible.
apply: Design for the two-week returner: can they resume without remembering anything?
Put help where the action is, not in a help center
Show current state explicitly; never require users to remember what they set
flags: Features reachable only if you remember they exist
Support tickets that start with 'I can't find…'
question: What does this screen assume the user remembers, and why would they?

learned: Peak-end rule: memory keeps the most intense moment and the final one, not the average. A smooth flow ending in a glitchy payment screen is remembered as a broken product. Disney engineers the exit. Endings are disproportionately cheap leverage — the last moment is what gets retold, reviewed, and re-purchased.
apply: Map each journey's actual end (confirmation, export, cancel, goodbye) and design it deliberately
Find the intensity peak per flow; make sure it's positive, or soften it if negative
Spend disproportionate polish on completion states vs. middles
when: Error states near the end of long flows
flags: Confirmation screens that are afterthoughts
Perfect funnel, terrible receipt
question: What is the very last thing this flow makes the user feel?

learned: Goal-gradient: effort rises as the finish line nears, and even artificial progress works — LinkedIn's pre-filled '25% complete' moved millions to finish profiles. Never start users at zero. The design question isn't whether to show progress, it's where the meter starts, how visible the next step is, and whether the end feels close.
apply: Start every progress indicator with credit already earned
Break long flows so the next milestone is always visibly near
Celebrate completion in proportion to effort invested
flags: Progress bars starting at 0% for signed-up users
Long flows with no visible milestones
question: Does the user feel like they're finishing something, or starting from nothing?

learned: Gestalt principles mean layout speaks before copy: spacing, alignment, and similarity announce what belongs together and what matters before a single word is read. If visual grouping contradicts logical grouping, the page lies and users misread prices, plans, and relationships. Google's results page is structure doing the explaining.
apply: Squint test every layout: do the visual clusters match the logical ones?
Use spacing as the primary grouping tool before boxes and dividers
Check that the visual hierarchy ranks what the business/user actually ranks
flags: Captions or labels needed to explain what proximity should have said
Users associating the right data with the wrong item
question: If nobody could read the words, what would the layout alone claim?

learned: Goal-setting theory: people organize behavior around outcomes, not tasks — nobody wants to 'complete the wizard', they want the change it promises. The milkshake was hired to survive a commute. Task completion can be 100% while goal attainment is zero, and users churn 'inexplicably'. Design serves the goal behind the task or it serves nothing.
apply: Write the user's actual goal at the top of every flow spec; check each step against it
Measure goal attainment (did the change happen?) alongside completion
Cut steps that serve our process rather than their outcome
flags: Perfect completion rates with poor retention
Steps that exist because the org chart needed them
question: What change in the user's life does this flow exist to cause, and does it?

learned: Present bias and hyperbolic discounting: value now is weighted absurdly higher than value later, so every minute between signup and first felt value bleeds users. Spotify plays music first, asks questions later. 'They'll love it once they set it up' is a graveyard sentence — move the feeling forward or lose the cohort.
apply: Measure minutes-to-first-value; treat it as a core metric
Deliver something delightful in session one, even if it's a preview of the real value
Defer every setup question that can be answered later
flags: Value arriving in session three
Setup wizard before any payoff
question: What does the user feel in the first ninety seconds that makes them want a second session?

learned: The intention-behavior gap: people honestly mean what they say in the interview and still do the opposite in real life, where friction and habit vote. Jawbone owners swore commitment and quietly stopped. Stated intent is a hypothesis, not a forecast — behavior with stakes is the only currency research should settle in.
apply: Discount stated willingness by default; require behavioral evidence for big bets
Prototype with real stakes: pre-orders, deposits, actual usage over promises
Instrument the real behavior post-launch and compare against what research predicted
flags: '87% said they would use it' as a green light
Research participants performing their ideal self
question: What did users do with real stakes attached — not what did they say?

learned: Affective primacy: the feeling arrives before the reasoning, and the reasoning is often the feeling explaining itself. Clippy was dead at the first eyebrow. Users call a dashboard 'unprofessional' and can't say why; the why is downstream of a gut reaction to something specific. Fix the trigger, not the explanation.
apply: Capture the first emotional word in every test, before any discussion
Treat unexplainable dislike as data pointing at a concrete trigger — hunt for it
Design the first three seconds of each surface for the feeling you want
flags: Dismissing feedback because users 'can't articulate it'
Rebutting feelings with feature lists
question: What does this screen make someone feel before they understand it?

learned: Loss aversion, status quo bias, and reactance gang up on every redesign: users judge it not on merit but against what was taken, and losses hit roughly twice as hard as gains. Snapchat broke muscle memory overnight and paid publicly. Redesigns aren't launches, they're negotiations with people's habits.
apply: Run a loss audit pre-ship: everything a habitual user reaches for that moved or died
Migrate progressively; keep escape hatches and familiar paths during transition
Frame changes in terms of what's preserved, then what's gained
when: Any redesign of a habit-formed product
Deprecating features with small loyal audiences
flags: Internal excitement with zero migration plan
'They'll adjust in a week' as the strategy
question: What exactly are we taking away, from whom, and what's their first hour like?

learned: Cultural cognition: clarity, trust, and premium are cultural verdicts, not properties of the pixels. The sparse checkout reads elegant in Berlin and suspiciously empty in Tokyo; WeChat and WhatsApp solve identical jobs with opposite instincts. I learned one culture's defaults and shipped them as universal truths — every export market deserves its own 'what feels normal here'.
apply: Test with people who grew up in the target market, not expats or translators
Ask 'what feels normal/trustworthy here', never 'do you like our design'
Audit density, color meanings, payment expectations, and social features per market
flags: 'Just translate it' as the launch plan
One market's conventions labeled 'best practice'
question: Which culture's definition of 'clear' did we build in, and does the target market share it?

learned: The endowment effect plus habit loops mean the incumbent product isn't chosen daily — it runs without thought, and ownership alone inflates its value. Betamax lost to what people already had. Winning the comparison table is irrelevant if switching feels like starting over; the real competitor is the user's autopilot.
apply: Study the old behavior's first ten seconds before designing your alternative
Make switching feel like continuing: import data, mirror shortcuts, honor old habits
Reward the first sessions heavily — that's when the old loop pulls hardest
flags: Strategy decks comparing features, not habits
No import path from the incumbent
question: What does the user's current autopilot give them in ten seconds that we ask them to rebuild?

learned: Hick's law and feature fatigue: every added option slows choice and every added feature is a decision charged to the user, compounding into a product made of homework — Jira became the cautionary tale. People overbuy capability and abandon complexity. The comparison table rewards more; the Tuesday-afternoon experience rewards less.
apply: Count user-facing decisions per screen and flow; budget and enforce
Ship features with defaults so good most users never open the settings
Say no upstream: every sales-driven option request gets a complexity cost estimate
flags: Setup time growing quarter over quarter
Features used by 2% blocking clarity for 98%
question: How many decisions does a new user make before their first success, and which three can we delete?

learned: Selective attention is brutal: the brain filters out anything not obviously relevant right now, including the banner above the fold that Facebook proved nobody saw. Attention isn't bought by size or color wars — that arms race mutes everything. It's bought by relevance, timing, and sparsity: one message, where the eyes already are, when it matters.
apply: One primary message per moment; everything else yields
Place communication in the user's task path, not beside it
Time announcements to relevance (feature promo when the need arises, not at login)
flags: Banner blindness in your own heatmaps
Louder assets requested to fix ignored assets
question: Why would a user mid-task consider this worth their attention right now?

learned: Learned helplessness is the quietest churn: after repeated dead-end failures, users stop trying even when they still want the outcome — no complaint, no ticket, just gone. Only six people got through in the study; the rest sat in the noise. Every vague error without a next step teaches 'effort here is wasted'. The lesson compounds.
apply: Find the first moment your product lets someone fail with no way forward; fix that first
Every error gets a next step: a fix, an alternative, or a human
Watch rage-clicks and repeated failures in analytics as pre-churn signals
flags: 'Something went wrong' with no verb after it
Silent churn spikes after failure-prone flows
question: Where does our product teach users that trying is pointless?

learned: Authority bias: the boss's aside becomes the room's verdict, not because anyone decided but because power reweights evidence — Amazon teams shipped things Bezos merely mused about. The title changes the physics of the discussion. If I'm senior, my early opinion is contamination; if I'm not, my job is getting judgments recorded before the leaning starts.
apply: Collect written opinions from everyone before any senior voice speaks
As the senior person: speak last, ask for the strongest case against your lean
Separate 'the boss prefers' from 'the evidence says' explicitly in decision docs
flags: Room consensus forming within minutes of the VP's comment
Decision docs citing preferences, not evidence
question: Would this option still win if nobody knew who liked it?

learned: Meetings don't reveal what a team thinks — they manufacture it. Conformity filters doubts, polarization amplifies early leanings, and the group leaves more certain without being more right. New Coke passed every room it entered. The room is an instrument that needs calibrating: independent judgments first, structured dissent always.
apply: Gather independent written takes before discussion opens
Assign a rotating devil's advocate with real license
Track meeting-certainty vs. outcome quality; recalibrate the process when they diverge
flags: Unanimity arriving fast on a hard question
Doubters going quiet rather than convinced
question: Who left this meeting more certain, and did anyone leave more correct?

learned: The planning fallacy makes the rewrite look clean because the old system's scars are invisible from a distance, and not-invented-here makes the old system look worse than it is. Digg rebuilt itself away from what users loved and died of it. A redesign that can't name five verified problems it solves is avoidance wearing ambition's clothes.
apply: Require five specific, evidence-backed problems before approving any ground-up redesign
Estimate rewrites against your org's actual history, not the team's optimism
Prefer strangler-pattern incremental replacement over big-bang rewrites
when: New leadership wanting a new product skin
flags: The rewrite pitch has more energy than evidence
Nobody can list what the old version got right
question: Name five real, verified problems this rebuild solves — and what the old system quietly handled that we've forgotten.

learned: Goodhart's and Campbell's laws: the moment a metric becomes the target, it stops measuring what we cared about — teams optimize the number, not the user, and the dashboard rewards desperation the same as delight. Facebook found engagement counting outrage. Every KPI needs its worst-case story told before it steers anything.
apply: For each KPI, document the ugliest way it could go up; monitor for exactly that
Pair every growth metric with a counter-metric (engagement × complaint rate, DAU × uninstalls)
Periodically re-derive metrics from the user outcome they were meant to proxy
when: Evaluating A/B wins that feel off
flags: Number up, product intuition down
Teams gaming their own dashboards rationally
question: What's the worst user experience that would make this metric look great?

learned: Escalation of commitment: the more time, money, and reputation the roadmap absorbs, the more it starts doing the team's thinking — the FBI kept serving a project everyone knew was failing. Commitments harden into identity. Plans should be re-earned by evidence, not defended by tenure on the slide.
apply: Re-justify every major initiative quarterly as if proposing it fresh
Make killing a project reputationally safe — celebrate well-reasoned stops
Put 'what would make us stop' in every project brief at kickoff
flags: 'We've come too far' in any prioritization discussion
Roadmap items nobody can re-argue from user evidence
question: If this project were proposed today, with what we now know, would we fund it?

learned: Sunk cost fallacy ships broken things: 'we already spent eight months' is an argument about the past being used to bill the future — Microsoft shipped Kin anyway and killed it in 48 days. The spend is gone either way; the only live question is the user's next experience. Effort is not evidence of value.
apply: Evaluate ship decisions purely on the user's next experience, never the team's past effort
Give every big effort a pre-committed kill criterion at kickoff
Reframe 'wasted work' as tuition: what did it teach, and archive it well
flags: Budget spent cited as a reason to ship
'Let's just get it out and fix it later' on a known-broken thing
question: If this had cost us nothing, would we still ship it today?

learned: Organizational memory fades person by person: the reasoning leaves with people even when documents stay, and teams re-run old mistakes as fresh ideas — MySpace relearned its own lessons in public. Every departure is a small lobotomy unless the why gets written down, not just the what.
apply: Record decision rationale (context, options, why) in durable, searchable docs
Before any big initiative: 'has this org tried this before, and what happened?'
Run pre-mortems with the longest-tenured people in the room
when: Deprecating things whose original purpose is fuzzy
flags: 'Why did we do it this way?' met with silence
The same failed experiment proposed every 18 months
question: Who remembers why we decided this, and what happens when they leave?

learned: Problem framing is the biggest design decision: the frame decides what you notice, what you ignore, and which solutions are even visible. Segway solved personal transport brilliantly and answered a question nobody asked. Accepting the brief's frame too fast means solving the wrong problem very well — the most expensive kind of success.
apply: Rewrite every brief in the user's own words before ideating
Generate three distinct framings of the problem; pick deliberately
Revisit the frame mid-project — some problems change as you work them
when: Inheriting a brief from another team
When solutions feel forced or joyless
flags: Brief accepted verbatim from the requester
Great execution, lukewarm user response
question: What question is this project actually answering, and who is asking it?

learned: Motivated reasoning turns research into an alibi: once the team is attached to a direction, studies get designed, read, and presented to keep it alive. If every readout validates the roadmap, the readouts are broken. Rigor isn't a vibe, it's structural: criteria fixed before data, uninvested moderators, disconfirming evidence first.
apply: Fix success/failure criteria before any data collection
Let someone with no stake run the sessions and first-pass the analysis
Lead every readout with what didn't support the plan
flags: Studies that have never once said 'no'
Methodology adjusted until the answer improved
question: When did our research last change our mind about anything?

learned: Dark patterns are persuasive tech pointed against the user: fake urgency, guilt buttons, cancellation mazes. They convert this quarter and corrode trust for years — and now that I understand defaults, friction, and framing, ignorance is no longer available as an excuse. The knowledge in this book is a loaded tool; direction is my responsibility.
apply: Run the transparency test on every persuasive mechanic: would users thank us if they saw exactly how it works?
Make leaving as honest as joining: cancellation in ≤ the clicks of signup
Refuse fake scarcity/urgency outright; escalate if asked to build it
flags: Countdown timers not attached to real deadlines
Metrics improving while support sentiment sours
question: Would I be comfortable explaining this mechanic, out loud, to the user it's aimed at?
